food_subgroup: Fried chicken with sambal and side salad

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for Ayam Geprek; closest defensible match is 'fried chicken food product' (FOODON_03315116). Smashed preparation and sambal accompaniment are not captured.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Chicken, fried) is the closest defensible anchor. Ayam Geprek's smashed technique and sambal geprek component are not represented in FoodEx2 as a distinct entry.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Ayam Geprek or Indonesian smashed fried chicken as of 2026.

main_ingredients: bird's eye chilli (Devil's Chili / cabe rawit)

serving.description: 1 plate with fried chicken, sambal geprek, and side salad
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising one boneless deep-fried smashed chicken leg (roughly 180–200 g), a portion of sambal geprek (approximately 50–60 g), and a side salad of shredded cabbage and cucumber (approximately 80–100 g).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, leg, meat only, cooked, fried (SR Legacy ID 05066) and Chicken, broilers or fryers, fried, batter, leg (SR Legacy ID 05062), combined with HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data for sambal and side salad components
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Ayam Geprek as a composite dish (batter-fried smashed chicken leg + sambal geprek + side salad) does not appear as a single entry in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for batter-fried chicken leg (SR Legacy 05062) as the dominant component, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo data for sambal and vegetable side components. The input per_100g values (energy 214 kcal, protein 12.9 g, fat 13.7 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g) are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained with minor rounding adjustments. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for battered fried chicken
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ayam Geprek in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated using battered fried chicken as the closest analogous food (GI ~65, medium category, per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The wheat-flour batter and moderate carbohydrate content (10 g/100 g) support a medium GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 35.0 g) / 100 = 22.75, rounded to 22.8 g. Available carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 10.0 g/100 g × 3.5 = 35.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (342.9 mg/100 g; ~1,200 mg per 350 g serving) from sambal, batter seasoning, and marinade, combined with saturated fat from deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–180 g and request reduced salt in the sambal and batter, as a full 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,200 mg sodium — nearly 52% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (estimated 65) with moderate carbohydrate load (35 g per serving) from wheat-flour batter, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 22.8 per serving, which exceeds the low-GL threshold of 10
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit this dish to a half-portion (approximately 175 g) to keep glycemic load below 12, and pair it with additional non-starchy vegetables rather than white rice to moderate post-meal blood glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes saturated fat (4.3 g/100 g; ~15 g per serving) and trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), alongside high sodium (342.9 mg/100 g) and cholesterol (51.4 mg/100 g); advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) generated during deep-frying further promote vascular inflammation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its combined saturated fat, trans fat, sodium, and AGE burden; if consumed, a portion of no more than 150 g with sambal on the side and no added rice is advisable, and grilled or baked chicken preparations should be substituted where possible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); sambal contains nightshade ingredients (chilli, tomato) that may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations due to AGE formation and consider grilled or baked chicken alternatives; nightshade ingredients (chilli, tomato) in the sambal should be monitored for symptom flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Geprek is an Indonesian dish consisting of a boneless chicken leg that is coated in a seasoned wheat-flour batter, deep-fried until golden and crispy, then smashed flat and pressed into a freshly pounded raw sambal geprek made from bird's eye chillies (cabe rawit / Devil's Chili), garlic, shallots, tomato, and salt. The smashing technique fractures the crispy crust, allowing the fiery sambal to penetrate the meat. The dish is typically served with a simple side salad of shredded cabbage and sliced cucumber. Originating in Yogyakarta, Indonesia, Ayam Geprek has become widely popular across Southeast Asia and is commonly available on food delivery platforms in Singapore. The flavour profile is intensely spicy, savoury, and umami-rich, with a contrast between the crunchy battered exterior and the juicy chicken meat. Nutritionally, a 350 g serving provides approximately 749 kcal, 45 g protein, 48 g fat, and 35 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is high at approximately 1,200 mg per serving, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The medium glycemic index (estimated 65) and glycemic load of approximately 22.8 per serving are relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. Deep-frying contributes saturated fat (~15 g per serving) and generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are pro-inflammatory and relevant for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets and is dairy-free but contains gluten from the wheat-flour batter.

ingredient_synonyms.sambal geprek: raw pounded chilli paste with garlic and shallots

confidence_reason: Ayam Geprek as a composite dish (batter-fried smashed chicken leg + sambal geprek + side salad) does not appear as a single entry in any approved primary nutrient source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy batter-fried chicken leg data cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo component data. The input per_100g values are broadly consistent with this composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from battered fried chicken. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite Indonesian fried chicken dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.1 g/100 g based on the input source estimate; this is a trace level consistent with deep-frying in commercial oils. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from battered fried chicken per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Ayam Geprek exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. The input source listed (foodo.sg) was noted in the original record but the nutrient values were cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy batter-fried chicken leg (SR Legacy 05062) as the primary approved source. 5. This dish is classified as Indonesian cuisine; mandatory Indonesian local name is satisfied by 'Ayam Geprek'. The dish is also widely served in Singapore, so Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names have been provided to support multilingual retrieval in the Singapore context.
